metadata.title: Reflection Paper on Methodological Issues in Confirmatory Clinical Trials Planned with an Adaptive Design

rules.rule_text: Adaptations to confirmatory trials must be pre-planned; adaptations introduced without proper planning render the trial to be considered exploratory.
rules.verbatim_quote: In all instances the interim analysis and the type of the anticipated design modification (change of sample size, discontinuation of treatment arms, etc.) would need to be described and justified in the study protocol. Adaptations to confirmatory trials introduced without proper planning will render the trial to be considered exploratory.

rules.rule_text: An adaptive design must ensure that statistical methods control the pre-specified type I error, that correct estimates and confidence intervals for the treatment effect are available, and that methods for the assessment of homogeneity of results from different stages are pre-planned.
rules.verbatim_quote: Using an adaptive design implies that the statistical methods control the pre-specified type I error, that correct estimates and confidence intervals for the treatment effect are available, and that methods for the assessment of homogeneity of results from different stages are pre-planned.

rules.rule_text: A thorough discussion will be required to ensure that results from different stages can be justifiably combined.
rules.verbatim_quote: A thorough discussion will be required to ensure that results from different stages can be justifiably combined.

rules.rule_text: Adaptive designs should not be seen as a means to alleviate the burden of rigorous planning of clinical trials; they are best utilised as a tool for planning clinical trials in areas where it is necessary to cope with difficult experimental situations.
rules.verbatim_quote: adaptive designs should not be seen as a means to alleviate the burden of rigorous planning of clinical trials. Instead, adaptive designs would be best utilised as a tool for planning clinical trials in areas where it is necessary to cope with difficult experimental situations.

rules.rule_text: Interim data should be provided by an independent statistician to an independent decision-making committee; sponsor involvement is discouraged.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, interim data should be provided by an independent statistician to an independent decision-making committee (see Guideline on DMCs); sponsor involvement is discouraged.

rules.rule_text: At the planning stage of the trial, three important issues need to be considered: (i) is there a need to perform any interim analysis; (ii) is the number of interim analyses justified; and (iii) is the information flow carefully described and controlled.
rules.verbatim_quote: To minimise these risks, three important issues need to be considered during the planning stage of the study: (i) is there a need to perform any interim analysis? (ii) is the number of interim analyses justified? and (iii) is the information flow carefully described and controlled?

rules.rule_text: Routinely breaking the blind should be avoided particularly when it can be foreseen that insufficient information will be available for stopping the study because of proven efficacy, or futility, or meaningful safety concerns of the experimental treatment.
rules.verbatim_quote: Routinely breaking the blind should be avoided particularly when it can be foreseen that insufficient information will be available for stopping the study because of proven efficacy, or futility, or meaningful safety concerns of the experimental treatment.

rules.rule_text: From a regulatory point of view, any interim analysis without realistic objectives should be avoided when planning the trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, from a regulatory point of view, any interim analysis without realistic objectives should be avoided when planning the trial.

rules.rule_text: Decision making about stopping a trial early should also consider the fact that acceptance of study results is not only based on a statistically significant primary result; primary efficacy data should be complimented by a careful assessment of consistency of trial results beyond the primary variable(s), including results in important subgroups, and the adequacy of the safety database.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, decision making about stopping a trial early should also consider the fact that acceptance of study results is not only based on a statistically significant primary result. Primary efficacy data should be complimented by a careful assessment of consistency of trial results beyond the primary variable(s), including results in important subgroups, and the adequacy of the safety database.

rules.rule_text: A discussion is needed as to whether all the requisite information can be provided if the study is stopped at an interim analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: A discussion is needed as to whether all the requisite information can be provided if the study is stopped at an interim analysis.

rules.rule_text: It is accepted regulatory practice to base decision making on the final results of the trial (not the interim analysis) when overrunning patients are included; overrunning patients need to be treated and observed according to the protocol.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is accepted regulatory practice to base decision making on the final results of the trial (not the interim analysis). This is also in accordance with the intention to treat principle that all randomised patients should be analysed. Obviously, overrunning patients need to be treated and observed according to the protocol and due attention should be given to this at the planning stage of the trial.

rules.rule_text: In all cases, results including and excluding the overrunning patients should be presented and differences between these two analyses should be discussed.
rules.verbatim_quote: In all cases, results including and excluding the overrunning patients should be presented and differences between these two analyses should be discussed.

rules.rule_text: If design changes are anticipated in a confirmatory clinical trial, this requires pre-planning and a clear justification from an experimental point of view; limitations of the current knowledge should be outlined in the study protocol to justify the option to modify the design.
rules.verbatim_quote: If design changes are anticipated in a confirmatory clinical trial this would require pre-planning and a clear justification from an experimental point of view: limitations of the current knowledge (e.g. at the end of phase II) should be outlined in the study protocol to justify the option to modify the design.

rules.rule_text: Argumentation should be provided as to why an anticipated design modification can safely be incorporated into the study and why the interpretability of the trial is not endangered; a discussion would be required why it would not be more appropriate to resolve the outstanding issues in additional exploratory studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: Argumentation should be provided as to why an anticipated design modification can safely be incorporated into the study and why the interpretability of the trial is not endangered. A discussion would be required why it would not be more appropriate to resolve the outstanding issues in additional exploratory studies.

rules.rule_text: If an adaptive design is used, the number of design modifications should be limited.
rules.verbatim_quote: If an adaptive design is used, the number of design modifications should be limited.

rules.rule_text: A minimal prerequisite for statistical methods to be accepted in the regulatory setting is the control of the pre-specified type I error; corresponding methods to estimate the size of the treatment effect and to provide confidence intervals with pre-specified coverage probability are required in addition to the presentation of the P-value.
rules.verbatim_quote: A minimal prerequisite for statistical methods to be accepted in the regulatory setting is the control of the pre-specified type I error (for a definition see Point to Consider on multiplicity issues in clinical trials). Corresponding methods to estimate the size of the treatment effect and to provide confidence intervals with pre-specified coverage probability are required in addition to the presentation of the P-value.

rules.rule_text: Whenever trials are planned to incorporate design modifications based on the results of an interim analysis, the applicant must pre-plan methods to ensure that results from different stages of the trial can be justifiably combined; the justification should include consideration of the impact of the modification on patient population, schedule of assessments, patient management and other features of the trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: From a regulatory point of view, whenever trials are planned to incorporate design modifications based on the results of an interim analysis, the applicant must pre-plan methods to ensure that results from different stages of the trial can be justifiably combined. The justification should include consideration of the impact of the modification on patient population, schedule of assessments, patient management and other features of the trial, which, if affected, would complicate the interpretation of the trial.

rules.rule_text: Studies with adaptive designs need at least the same careful investigation of heterogeneity and justification to combine the results of different stages as is usually required for the combination of individual trials in a meta-analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: In this respect, studies with adaptive designs need at least the same careful investigation of heterogeneity and justification to combine the results of different stages as is usually required for the combination of individual trials in a meta-analysis.

rules.rule_text: Addressing potential issues about combining results from different stages should be done at the planning stage of the trial in order to avoid post-hoc discussions; trials should not be planned to make many changes along a series of small steps with limited numbers of patients at each step.
rules.verbatim_quote: Addressing such issues at the planning stage of the trial is essential to avoid post-hoc discussions about whether observed data may indicate that combining results from different stages of the trial is justified or not. Hence, trials should not be planned to make many changes along a series of small steps with limited numbers of patients at each step.

rules.rule_text: The relevance of a particular size of treatment effect should be discussed at the planning stage of the trial and not deferred to the point where interim results are already available; consideration of what magnitude of treatment effect might be of clinical importance should not be influenced by trial results (interim or final).
rules.verbatim_quote: The relevance of a particular size of treatment effect should be discussed at the planning stage of the trial and not deferred to the point where interim results are already available. In general, consideration of what magnitude of treatment effect might be of clinical importance should not be influenced by trial results (interim or final).

rules.rule_text: Analysis methods that control the type I error must be pre-specified; whenever possible, methods for blinded sample size reassessment that properly control the type I error should be used; in cases where sample size needs to be reassessed based on unblinded data, sufficient justification should be made.
rules.verbatim_quote: Analysis methods that control the type I error must be pre-specified. Whenever possible, methods for blinded sample size reassessment that properly control the type I error should be used, especially if the sole aim of the interim analysis is the re-calculation of sample size. In cases where sample size needs to be reassessed based on unblinded data, sufficient justification should be made.

rules.rule_text: In a confirmatory setting, effects must always be attributable to specific endpoints to clarify the capabilities of the drug treatment; the mere rejection of a global hypothesis combining results from different endpoints will not be sufficient as proof of efficacy.
rules.verbatim_quote: Furthermore, in a confirmatory setting, effects must always be attributable to specific endpoints to clarify the capabilities of the drug treatment. The mere rejection of a global hypothesis combining results from different endpoints will not be sufficient as proof of efficacy.

rules.rule_text: Potential implications regarding the patient population selected for inclusion after discontinuing a treatment arm should be discussed in the study protocol; a multiple testing procedure to identify the appropriate dose should be incorporated.
rules.verbatim_quote: The mere rejection of the global null hypothesis at the end of the trial is not usually sufficient as proof of efficacy: it is not sufficient to show that some dose of the experimental treatment is effective. In consequence, a multiple testing procedure to identify the appropriate dose should be incorporated.

rules.rule_text: Multiple testing procedures should be carefully developed and explained in the study protocol when doses are dropped at an interim analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: Multiple testing procedures should be carefully developed and explained in the study protocol.

rules.rule_text: Whenever a non-inferiority trial is planned, discussion of the assay sensitivity of the trial is of paramount importance and justification of a non-inferiority margin needs particular consideration.
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever a non-inferiority trial is planned, discussion of the assay sensitivity of the trial is of paramount importance and justification of a non-inferiority margin needs particular consideration (for principal considerations see: Points to Consider on switching between Superiority and Non-inferiority; Points to Consider on choice of the non-inferiority margin).

rules.rule_text: It is generally not acceptable in the adaptive framework to change the trial objective from superiority to non-inferiority (and to justify a non-inferiority margin) after interim results are available.
rules.verbatim_quote: Similarly it is generally not acceptable in the adaptive framework to change the trial objective from superiority to non-inferiority (and to justify a non-inferiority margin) after interim results are available.

rules.rule_text: If a trial is to continue after formal proof of non-inferiority at an interim analysis, final conclusions should be based on all data from all stages of the study, even if the final confidence interval is less supportive than the one obtained from the interim analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: If a trial is to continue after formal proof of non-inferiority at an interim analysis then any ambiguity in interpretation of results at the final analysis should be avoided by basing final conclusions on all data from all stages of the study, even if the final confidence interval is less supportive than the one obtained from the interim analysis.

rules.rule_text: For substantial changes to an ongoing trial introduced via formal protocol amendment, as a minimal requirement the primary analysis should be stratified according to whether patients were randomised before or after the protocol amendment and homogeneity of the results should be carefully investigated and discussed.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a minimal requirement the primary analysis should be stratified according to whether patients were randomised before or after the protocol amendment and homogeneity of the results should be carefully investigated and discussed.

rules.rule_text: Where substantial changes to an ongoing trial are made, the applicant will need to carefully argue why a combination of the results from different stages is capable of substantiating a final treatment recommendation and why the confirmatory nature of the trial is not damaged.
rules.verbatim_quote: In all such cases, the applicant will need to carefully argue why a combination of the results from different stages is capable of substantiating a final treatment recommendation and why the confirmatory nature of the trial is not damaged.

rules.rule_text: If a sponsor decides to continue a trial despite the fact that an interim analysis suggests stopping the trial for futility, the type I error rate is usually no longer controlled.
rules.verbatim_quote: If, however, a sponsor decides to continue a trial despite the fact that at an interim analysis suggest stopping the trial for futility, the type I error rate is usually no longer controlled.

rules.rule_text: Reasons for stopping a study have to be fully explained and understood; the overall type I error rate of the MAA may no longer be controlled if a study is stopped although pre-specified stopping criteria are not met and a new study is started thereafter.
rules.verbatim_quote: Similarly, the overall type I error rate of the MAA (that is, after all the trials have been completed) may also no longer be controlled if a study is stopped, although pre-specified stopping criteria are not met, and a new study is started thereafter. As always, reasons for stopping a study have to be fully explained and understood.
